1:
"Therefore, my brethren dearly beloved and longed for, my joy and crown, so stand fast in the Lord, my dearly beloved." The witnesses note Paul's tender call to stand fast, the Philippians his joy and crown. On the affectionate address, the grammatical voices note the warmth. Barnes and Robertson note the heaped-up terms of endearment: "my brethren dearly beloved and longed for (yearned after), my joy and crown" — the Philippians are Paul's joy now and his "crown" (stephanos — the victor's wreath, his reward and boast) at the day of Christ.

On "my joy and crown," the witnesses note the reward. Barnes and Calvin note that converts are the minister's joy and crown — his glory and reward; Paul would present them to Christ as the fruit of his labour, his garland of victory.

On "so stand fast in the Lord," the puritan Henry notes the charge: drawing the conclusion ("therefore," from the heavenly hope of ch. 3) Paul exhorts them to "stand fast (stand firm, hold your ground) in the Lord" — steadfast in faith and in union with Christ. The verse displays Paul's tender call to stand fast.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the heaped-up endearments — "my brethren dearly beloved and longed for (yearned after), my joy and crown," the Philippians Paul's joy now and his crown (victor's wreath, reward) at Christ's day (Barnes, Robertson); converts being the minister's joy and crown, his garland of victory (Barnes, Calvin); the charge "so stand fast in the Lord," steadfast in faith and union with Christ (Henry). The verse displays the call to stand fast. The verse marks the affection: dearly beloved. The verse displays the reward: my joy and crown. The verse displays the charge: stand fast. The verse displays the union: in the Lord. The call displays Paul tenderly urging steadfastness. The verse displays the brethren: beloved. The verse displays the crown: his reward. The verse displays the standing: fast. Paul's tender call to stand fast, the Philippians his joy and crown, displays the love that frames his exhortation: addressing them with the warmest endearment — his beloved, his joy, his victor's crown — he urges them to stand firm in the Lord, steadfast in faith and union with Christ. The verse displays the affectionate charge to steadfastness, applied to two who differed (v.2).
2:
"I beseech Euodias, and beseech Syntyche, that they be of the same mind in the Lord." The brief verse notes Paul's plea to two women to be of one mind. The grammatical voices note the personal appeal. Barnes and Robertson note Paul names two women of the church: "I beseech (entreat) Euodias, and beseech Syntyche, that they be of the same mind (think the same thing, agree) in the Lord" — evidently two prominent members who had fallen into some disagreement.

On "be of the same mind in the Lord," the witnesses note the reconciliation. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's tender, even-handed appeal — naming each separately ("beseech Euodias, and beseech Syntyche"), pleading equally with both to be reconciled and united "in the Lord," on the common ground of their faith.

The puritan Henry notes the delicacy: Paul does not take sides or rebuke harshly but entreats both, with equal affection, to lay aside their difference for the sake of their union in Christ. The verse displays Paul's plea to two women to be of one mind.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul names two women — "I beseech Euodias, and beseech Syntyche, that they be of the same mind (agree) in the Lord," prominent members in some disagreement (Barnes, Robertson); his even-handed appeal, naming each separately, pleading equally with both to be reconciled "in the Lord" (Barnes, Calvin); not taking sides but entreating both with equal affection (Henry). The verse displays the plea for unity. The verse marks the women: Euodias and Syntyche. The verse displays the plea: be of one mind. The verse displays the ground: in the Lord. The verse displays the delicacy: even-handed. The plea displays Paul reconciling two who differed. The verse displays the beseeching: of each. The verse displays the agreement: sought. The verse displays the Lord: the ground. Paul's plea to two women to be of one mind displays his pastoral care for unity even in a beloved church: naming Euodias and Syntyche each with equal tenderness, he entreats them to lay aside their difference and be of the same mind in the Lord. The verse displays the call to reconciliation, with a request for help (v.3).
3:
"And I intreat thee also, true yokefellow, help those women which laboured with me in the gospel, with Clement also, and with other my fellowlabourers, whose names are in the book of life." The witnesses note Paul's request to help the women, his fellow-labourers in the book of life. On "I intreat thee also, true yokefellow," the grammatical voices note the appeal. Barnes and Robertson note Paul asks a third party — "true yokefellow (gnesie syzyge — genuine companion in labour)" — to help reconcile the two women; the identity of this yokefellow is uncertain (some have taken syzygos as a proper name).

On "help those women which laboured with me in the gospel," the witnesses note their service. Barnes and Calvin note that these women had "laboured with me (striven side by side) in the gospel" — they had been Paul's fellow-workers in the cause; their disagreement is the more to be healed because of their past service.

On "with Clement... whose names are in the book of life," the grammatical-historical note the fellowship: with "Clement also and other my fellowlabourers, whose names are in the book of life" — the register of the saved; Paul commends these faithful workers, assured that, whatever their present difference, their names are written in heaven. The verse displays Paul's request to help the women, his fellow-labourers in the book of life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul asks a third party — "true yokefellow (genuine companion in labour)" — to help reconcile the two women, the yokefellow's identity uncertain (Barnes, Robertson); these women had "laboured with me in the gospel," Paul's fellow-workers, their disagreement the more to be healed (Barnes, Calvin); "with Clement and other fellowlabourers, whose names are in the book of life," the register of the saved (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the request to help them. The verse marks the appeal: true yokefellow. The verse displays the women: fellow-labourers. The verse displays the names: in the book of life. The verse displays the assurance: written in heaven. The request displays Paul commending his faithful fellow-workers. The verse displays the yokefellow: entreated. The verse displays the labour: in the gospel. The verse displays the book: of life. Paul's request to help the women, his fellow-labourers in the book of life, displays his care to heal their breach: he asks a true companion to aid in reconciling Euodias and Syntyche — women who had laboured with him in the gospel, with Clement and others whose names are written in the book of life. The verse displays the honour of faithful workers, framed by the call to constant joy (v.4).
4:
"Rejoice in the Lord alway: and again I say, Rejoice." The witnesses note the repeated call to rejoice in the Lord always. On "rejoice in the Lord alway," the grammatical voices note the command. Barnes and Robertson note Paul repeats the keynote (3:1): "rejoice in the Lord alway (at all times, in every circumstance)" — Christian joy is to be constant, not dependent on changing conditions, for its source is the unchanging Lord.

On "in the Lord," the eastern Chrysostom and eastern Cyril note the ground: the joy is "in the Lord" — grounded in Christ, who is always the same; therefore it can be "alway," even in suffering and want, for the Lord never fails.

On "and again I say, Rejoice," the Reformed Calvin notes the emphasis: Paul repeats the command for stress — "again I say, Rejoice" — so important and so contrary to nature (in affliction) is this duty that he doubles it, that they may not miss it. The verse displays the repeated call to rejoice in the Lord always.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul repeats the keynote — "rejoice in the Lord alway (at all times)," Christian joy constant, not dependent on conditions, its source the unchanging Lord (Barnes, Robertson); the joy "in the Lord," grounded in Christ who is always the same, so it can be always, even in suffering (Chrysostom, Cyril); "and again I say, Rejoice," doubled for emphasis, so important and contrary to nature in affliction (Calvin). The verse displays constant joy. The verse marks the command: rejoice alway. The verse displays the ground: in the Lord. The verse displays the constancy: at all times. The verse displays the emphasis: again I say. The command displays joy grounded in the unchanging Lord. The verse displays the rejoicing: always. The verse displays the Lord: its source. The verse displays the repetition: for stress. The repeated call to rejoice in the Lord always displays the heart of the epistle of joy: Paul commands a constant gladness grounded not in circumstances but in the unchanging Lord — and doubles the command, "again I say, Rejoice," that even in suffering and want the believer may not fail to rejoice. The verse displays the constant joy of the believer, joined to gentleness (v.5).
5:
"Let your moderation be known unto all men. The Lord is at hand." The brief verse notes the call to known gentleness, for the Lord is at hand. The grammatical voices note the grace. Barnes and Robertson note the charge: "let your moderation (epieikes — gentleness, forbearance, reasonableness, the yielding, considerate spirit that does not insist on its own rights) be known unto all men" — believers are to be marked by a gentle, forbearing temper, evident to all.

On "moderation," the witnesses note the breadth. Barnes and Calvin note the rich word: it includes patience under wrong, mildness, fairness, a willingness to yield — the opposite of a harsh, contentious, self-assertive spirit; this gentleness is to be shown to "all men," even opponents.

On "the Lord is at hand," the grammatical-historical note the motive: "the Lord is at hand (near)" — whether in his constant presence or his soon return; this nearness is the motive for gentleness — knowing the Lord is near to help and to judge, the believer can afford to be forbearing. The verse displays the call to known gentleness.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the charge "let your moderation (gentleness, forbearance, the yielding spirit) be known unto all men," believers marked by a gentle temper evident to all (Barnes, Robertson); the rich word including patience under wrong, mildness, fairness, the opposite of contention, shown even to opponents (Barnes, Calvin); "the Lord is at hand (near)," his nearness the motive for gentleness (grammatical-historical). The verse displays known gentleness. The verse marks the charge: moderation known. The verse displays the breadth: forbearance, fairness. The verse displays the scope: to all men. The verse displays the motive: the Lord is at hand. The call displays believers marked by gentle forbearance. The verse displays the moderation: gentle. The verse displays the men: all. The verse displays the Lord: at hand. The call to known gentleness, for the Lord is at hand, displays the grace that should mark the believer: a forbearing, considerate, yielding spirit, evident to all men even to opponents — fitting for those who know the Lord is near, to help and to judge. The verse displays the gentleness of the believer, freed from anxiety by prayer (vv.6-7).
6:
"Be careful for nothing; but in every thing by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known unto God." The witnesses note the call to anxious for nothing but prayerful in everything. On "be careful for nothing," the grammatical voices note the freedom from anxiety. Barnes and Robertson note the charge: "be careful (anxious, full of care, distracted with worry) for nothing" — believers are not to be consumed by anxiety about anything; the antidote to worry follows.

On "in every thing by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving," the witnesses note the remedy. Barnes and Calvin note the prescribed remedy: "in every thing (in all circumstances) by prayer (general devotion) and supplication (specific petition) with thanksgiving" — every care is to be turned into prayer, joined with gratitude for mercies already received.

On "let your requests be made known unto God," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the relief: "let your requests be made known unto God" — believers are to spread their needs before God, casting their cares on him; the burden carried to God in prayer is the cure for anxiety. The verse displays the call to anxious for nothing but prayerful in everything.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the charge "be careful (anxious, worried) for nothing," not consumed by anxiety (Barnes, Robertson); the remedy "in every thing by prayer (devotion) and supplication (petition) with thanksgiving," every care turned to prayer joined with gratitude (Barnes, Calvin); "let your requests be made known unto God," spreading needs before God, the cure for anxiety (Wesley). The verse displays prayer for anxiety. The verse marks the freedom: careful for nothing. The verse displays the remedy: prayer and supplication. The verse displays the gratitude: with thanksgiving. The verse displays the relief: requests to God. The call displays anxiety replaced by thankful prayer. The verse displays the care: forbidden. The verse displays the prayer: prescribed. The verse displays the requests: to God. The call to anxious for nothing but prayerful in everything displays the divine remedy for worry: instead of being consumed by anxiety about anything, the believer is in everything to turn his cares into prayer and thankful supplication, making his requests known to God. The verse displays the prayer that banishes anxiety, issuing in the peace of God (v.7).
7:
"And the peace of God, which passeth all understanding, shall keep your hearts and minds through Christ Jesus." The witnesses note the peace of God that passes understanding guarding the heart. On "the peace of God, which passeth all understanding," the grammatical voices note the promise. Barnes and Robertson note the result of prayer: "the peace of God (the tranquillity God gives, or the peace God himself possesses and imparts), which passeth (surpasses, transcends) all understanding (all human comprehension)" — a peace beyond what the mind can grasp or explain.

On "passeth all understanding," the witnesses note its greatness. Barnes and Calvin note that this peace surpasses understanding — either too great for the mind to comprehend, or able to guard the soul better than all human reasoning and planning can; a peace that the world cannot give or understand.

On "shall keep your hearts and minds through Christ Jesus," the Reformed Calvin notes the guarding: this peace "shall keep (phrouresei — garrison, guard, as a military sentinel) your hearts and minds (thoughts) through Christ Jesus" — like a garrison protecting a city, God's peace stands sentinel over the believer's heart and thoughts, keeping them secure in Christ. The verse displays the peace of God that passes understanding guarding the heart.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the result of prayer — "the peace of God, which passeth (surpasses) all understanding," a peace beyond human comprehension (Barnes, Robertson); surpassing understanding, too great for the mind or better than all human reasoning, which the world cannot give (Barnes, Calvin); it "shall keep (garrison, guard, as a sentinel) your hearts and minds through Christ Jesus," standing guard over heart and thoughts (Calvin). The verse displays the guarding peace. The verse marks the peace: of God. The verse displays the greatness: passeth understanding. The verse displays the guarding: keeps the heart. The verse displays the means: through Christ. The peace displays God's peace garrisoning the believer's heart. The verse displays the peace: surpassing. The verse displays the keeping: of heart and mind. The verse displays the Christ: the means. The peace of God that passes understanding guarding the heart displays the promised fruit of prayer: a peace beyond all human comprehension, given by God, which stands sentinel like a garrison over the believer's heart and thoughts, keeping them secure through Christ Jesus. The verse displays the guarding peace of God, leading to the cultivation of virtue (v.8).
8:
"Finally, brethren, whatsoever things are true, whatsoever things are honest, whatsoever things are just, whatsoever things are pure, whatsoever things are lovely, whatsoever things are of good report; if there be any virtue, and if there be any praise, think on these things." The witnesses note the call to think on whatsoever things are excellent. On the catalogue of virtues, the grammatical voices note the comprehensiveness. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's sweeping charge: "whatsoever things are true (truthful, sincere), honest (honourable, dignified, venerable), just (righteous, fair), pure (chaste, holy), lovely (amiable, winsome), of good report (well-spoken-of, of fair repute)" — the whole compass of moral excellence.

On "if there be any virtue, and if there be any praise," the witnesses note the summary. Barnes and Calvin note Paul gathers all up: "if there be any virtue (moral excellence) and any praise (anything praiseworthy)" — whatever in any quarter is good and commendable falls under the charge.

On "think on these things," the grammatical-historical note the command: "think on (logizesthe — consider, ponder, take account of, let the mind dwell on) these things" — the believer is to fill his mind with what is good and noble; the thoughts are to be governed and fed by these excellences. The verse displays the call to think on whatsoever things are excellent.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's sweeping catalogue — "true (sincere), honest (honourable), just (righteous), pure (chaste), lovely (winsome), of good report (well-spoken-of)," the whole compass of moral excellence (Barnes, Robertson); gathered up in "any virtue (moral excellence) and any praise (praiseworthy thing)," whatever is good and commendable (Barnes, Calvin); "think on (ponder, dwell on) these things," filling the mind with what is good (grammatical-historical). The verse displays thinking on the excellent. The verse marks the catalogue: true, honest, just, pure. The verse displays the summary: virtue and praise. The verse displays the command: think on these. The verse displays the aim: the mind governed by good. The call displays the believer feeding the mind on excellence. The verse displays the virtues: listed. The verse displays the praise: any. The verse displays the thinking: on these. The call to think on whatsoever things are excellent displays the discipline of the Christian mind: the believer is to ponder and dwell on all that is true, honourable, just, pure, lovely, and of good report — whatever is virtuous and praiseworthy — filling and governing the mind with what is good. The verse displays the cultivation of holy thought, joined to holy practice (v.9).
9:
"Those things, which ye have both learned, and received, and heard, and seen in me, do: and the God of peace shall be with you." The brief verse notes the call to do what they learned of Paul, with the promise of God's presence. The grammatical voices note the practice. Barnes and Robertson note that to right thinking (v.8) Paul adds right doing: "those things, which ye have both learned (by instruction), and received (accepted as a tradition), and heard (from report), and seen in me (in my example), do (practise)" — let their conduct match what they were taught and saw in him.

On the fourfold source, the witnesses note the pattern. Barnes and Calvin note that Paul appeals to his own teaching and example as the pattern: what they learned, received, heard, and saw in him is the model for their practice; doctrine and example together.

On "the God of peace shall be with you," the eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the promise: to the peace of God (v.7) Paul now adds "the God of peace shall be with you" — the very God who gives peace will himself be present with those who so live; his presence the reward of obedience. The verse displays the call to do what they learned of Paul.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: to right thinking Paul adds right doing — "those things, which ye have learned, received, heard, and seen in me, do (practise)," conduct matching teaching and example (Barnes, Robertson); Paul's teaching and example the pattern, doctrine and example together (Barnes, Calvin); "the God of peace shall be with you," the God of peace himself present with the obedient (Cyril, Calvin). The verse displays doing and God's presence. The verse marks the practice: do these things. The verse displays the source: learned and seen in Paul. The verse displays the promise: God of peace with you. The verse displays the reward: his presence. The call displays believers practising what they learned, with God present. The verse displays the learning: from Paul. The verse displays the doing: required. The verse displays the God: of peace. The call to do what they learned of Paul, with the promise of God's presence, displays the union of holy thought and holy practice: the believer is to put into practice all he learned, received, heard, and saw in the apostle — and to such the God of peace himself will be present. The verse displays the presence of the God of peace, the seal of the exhortation, before Paul's thanks for their gift (vv.10-20).
10:
"But I rejoiced in the Lord greatly, that now at the last your care of me hath flourished again; wherein ye were also careful, but ye lacked opportunity." The witnesses note Paul's joy at the renewal of the Philippians' care for him. On "I rejoiced in the Lord greatly, that now at the last your care of me hath flourished again," the grammatical voices note the gladness. Barnes and Robertson note Paul turns to thank them for their gift: "I rejoiced in the Lord greatly that now at the last your care (thoughtfulness) of me hath flourished again (revived, blossomed afresh, like a plant in spring)" — their renewed care gladdened him.

On "wherein ye were also careful, but ye lacked opportunity," the witnesses note the gracious explanation. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's tactful care not to imply they had neglected him: "ye were also careful (mindful, concerned)" all along, "but ye lacked opportunity (no occasion or means presented itself to send)" — their care had not failed, only the chance to express it.

The eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the delicacy: Paul rejoices in their love and tenderly excuses any seeming delay, attributing it not to coldness but to want of opportunity. The verse displays Paul's joy at the renewal of their care.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul thanks them — "I rejoiced in the Lord greatly that now at the last your care of me hath flourished again (revived, like a plant in spring)," their renewed care gladdening him (Barnes, Robertson); his tact in adding "ye were also careful (mindful) but ye lacked opportunity," their care not failing, only the chance to express it (Barnes, Calvin); tenderly excusing any seeming delay (Chrysostom, Henry). The verse displays joy at their care. The verse marks the joy: rejoiced greatly. The verse displays the renewal: flourished again. The verse displays the tact: ye lacked opportunity. The verse displays the delicacy: no coldness. The joy displays Paul glad at their revived care. The verse displays the rejoicing: in the Lord. The verse displays the care: renewed. The verse displays the opportunity: lacked. Paul's joy at the renewal of the Philippians' care for him displays the warmth of his gratitude and his tact: he rejoiced greatly that their thoughtful care had blossomed afresh — graciously adding that their concern had never failed, only the opportunity to show it. The verse displays the apostle's thankful joy, tempered by holy contentment (v.11).
11:
"Not that I speak in respect of want: for I have learned, in whatsoever state I am, therewith to be content." The witnesses note Paul's contentment in every state. On "not that I speak in respect of want," the grammatical voices note the disclaimer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul guards his thanks from any appearance of complaint or begging: "not that I speak in respect of want (not because I was in need, or am hinting for more)" — his joy was in their love, not in relief of his necessity.

On "I have learned, in whatsoever state I am, therewith to be content," the witnesses note the lesson. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's hard-won lesson: "I have learned (it was a thing learned, by experience and grace) in whatsoever state (circumstance) I am, therewith to be content (autarkes — self-sufficient, satisfied, independent of outward conditions)" — he had learned a contentment that rests not on circumstances but on God.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the discipline: contentment is not natural but learned — a grace acquired through trials, by which the soul is satisfied in God whatever the outward lot. The verse displays Paul's contentment in every state.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul guards his thanks — "not that I speak in respect of want (not from need, nor hinting for more)," his joy in their love not in relief (Barnes, Robertson); his hard-won lesson, "I have learned, in whatsoever state I am, therewith to be content (self-sufficient, satisfied, independent of conditions)," resting on God not circumstances (Barnes, Calvin); contentment a grace learned through trials (Wesley). The verse displays contentment. The verse marks the disclaimer: not from want. The verse displays the lesson: learned to be content. The verse displays the state: whatsoever. The verse displays the ground: satisfied in God. The contentment displays Paul independent of outward conditions. The verse displays the want: not the cause. The verse displays the contentment: learned. The verse displays the state: every one. Paul's contentment in every state displays the grace he had learned: he speaks not from want, for he had learned, in whatever circumstance, to be content — a satisfaction resting not on outward conditions but on God, acquired through long experience and trial. The verse displays the learned contentment of the apostle, in abundance and in want (v.12).
12:
"I know both how to be abased, and I know how to abound: every where and in all things I am instructed both to be full and to be hungry, both to abound and to suffer need." The brief verse notes Paul's learned mastery of plenty and want. The grammatical voices note the range. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's experience of both extremes: "I know both how to be abased (brought low, in poverty and humiliation) and I know how to abound (have plenty)" — he had tasted both ends of the scale and learned how to bear each.

On "I am instructed both to be full and to be hungry," the witnesses note the secret learned. Barnes and Calvin note the figure: "every where and in all things I am instructed (memyemai — initiated, as into a secret or mystery) both to be full and to be hungry, both to abound and to suffer need" — Paul had been initiated into the secret of contentment in every condition, full or empty.

On the balance, the grammatical-historical note the lesson: both abundance and want have their own temptations, and Paul had learned to meet each rightly — neither lifted up by plenty nor cast down by want. The verse displays Paul's learned mastery of plenty and want.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's experience of both extremes — "I know both how to be abased (brought low) and how to abound," tasting both ends and bearing each (Barnes, Robertson); "I am instructed (initiated, as into a secret) both to be full and to be hungry," initiated into the secret of contentment in every condition (Barnes, Calvin); both plenty and want having their temptations, met rightly (grammatical-historical). The verse displays mastery of plenty and want. The verse marks the abasement: how to be low. The verse displays the abounding: how to have plenty. The verse displays the secret: initiated. The verse displays the balance: full or hungry. The mastery displays Paul content in every condition. The verse displays the abased: borne. The verse displays the abounding: borne. The verse displays the instruction: learned. Paul's learned mastery of plenty and want displays the depth of his contentment: he had been initiated into the secret of bearing both abasement and abundance, fulness and hunger — content in every condition, neither lifted up by plenty nor cast down by need. The verse displays the apostle's all-sufficiency in Christ, summed in his great confession (v.13).
13:
"I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me." The witnesses note Paul's all-sufficiency through the strength of Christ. On "I can do all things," the grammatical voices note the claim. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's confident declaration: "I can do all things (I have strength for all things, I am equal to anything)" — not a boast of independent power, but of an ability that meets every demand.

On "through Christ which strengtheneth me," the witnesses note the source. Barnes and Calvin note the all-important qualification: "through Christ (in Christ, the one strengthening me) which strengtheneth me (endynamounti — pours strength into me, empowers me)" — the strength is not Paul's own but Christ's, infused into him; he can do all things only as Christ enables.

On the scope, the eastern Cyril notes the context: the "all things" here is chiefly the bearing of all conditions — abasement and abundance, fulness and want (v.12) — for which Christ gives sufficient strength; the believer is equal to every circumstance through Christ. The verse displays Paul's all-sufficiency through the strength of Christ.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's confident "I can do all things (I am equal to anything)," not independent power but ability meeting every demand (Barnes, Robertson); "through Christ which strengtheneth me (pours strength into me)," the strength Christ's not his own, infused into him (Barnes, Calvin); the "all things" chiefly bearing all conditions, for which Christ gives strength (Cyril). The verse displays all-sufficiency in Christ. The verse marks the claim: I can do all things. The verse displays the source: through Christ. The verse displays the strength: he infuses. The verse displays the scope: every condition. The confession displays Paul equal to all through Christ's power. The verse displays the ability: for all. The verse displays the Christ: strengthening. The verse displays the sufficiency: his. Paul's all-sufficiency through the strength of Christ displays the secret of his contentment: he is equal to all things — to abasement and abundance alike — not by his own power but through Christ, who pours strength into him; the believer can meet every circumstance in the strength his Lord supplies. The verse displays the believer's strength in Christ, that yet welcomed the Philippians' help (v.14).
14:
"Notwithstanding ye have well done, that ye did communicate with my affliction." The brief verse notes Paul's commendation of their sharing in his affliction. The grammatical voices note the praise. Barnes and Robertson note that, having declared his contentment, Paul is careful not to seem to slight their gift: "notwithstanding (yet) ye have well done (acted rightly, kindly) that ye did communicate with (share, have fellowship in) my affliction" — their gift was a sharing in his trouble.

On "communicate with my affliction," the witnesses note the fellowship. Barnes and Calvin note that to "communicate with his affliction" was to make his trouble their own, to enter into fellowship with him in his need by their bounty; their gift was an act of sympathy and partnership.

The grammatical-historical note the balance: though Paul was content and needed nothing, he gladly acknowledges that they did well — their kindness was right and commendable, a true fellowship in his suffering. The verse displays Paul's commendation of their sharing in his affliction.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, careful not to slight their gift, commends them — "ye have well done that ye did communicate with (share in) my affliction," their gift a sharing in his trouble (Barnes, Robertson); to "communicate with his affliction" being to make his trouble their own, an act of sympathy and partnership (Barnes, Calvin); though content, Paul gladly acknowledges they did well (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the commendation. The verse marks the praise: ye have well done. The verse displays the act: shared his affliction. The verse displays the fellowship: partnership in need. The verse displays the balance: content yet thankful. The commendation displays the Philippians sharing Paul's trouble. The verse displays the doing: well. The verse displays the affliction: shared. The verse displays the kindness: commended. Paul's commendation of their sharing in his affliction displays the grace of his thankfulness: though content and needing nothing, he gladly acknowledges that the Philippians did well in sharing his affliction by their gift — a true fellowship and sympathy in his need. The verse displays the partnership of their gift, grounded in their long faithfulness (vv.15-16).
15:
"Now ye Philippians know also, that in the beginning of the gospel, when I departed from Macedonia, no church communicated with me as concerning giving and receiving, but ye only." The brief verse notes the Philippians alone supporting Paul from the first. The grammatical voices note their distinction. Barnes and Robertson note Paul recalls their early and unique generosity: "in the beginning of the gospel (when it was first preached among them), when I departed from Macedonia, no church communicated with me (entered into partnership with me) as concerning giving and receiving, but ye only" — they alone supported him.

On "giving and receiving," the witnesses note the mutual account. Barnes and Calvin note the commercial figure: "giving and receiving" — as in a ledger of accounts; the Philippians gave material support, and Paul (and the gospel) gave spiritual blessing in return; a mutual partnership.

The eastern Cyril notes the singularity: of all the churches, only the Philippians had this fellowship with Paul in giving from the very first; their generosity was unique and steadfast. The verse displays the Philippians alone supporting Paul from the first.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul recalls their early, unique generosity — "in the beginning of the gospel, when I departed from Macedonia, no church communicated with me (partnered with me) as concerning giving and receiving, but ye only" (Barnes, Robertson); the commercial figure "giving and receiving" (a ledger), they giving material support and receiving spiritual blessing, a mutual partnership (Barnes, Calvin); only the Philippians having this fellowship from the first (Cyril). The verse displays their unique support. The verse marks the time: the beginning of the gospel. The verse displays the singularity: ye only. The verse displays the figure: giving and receiving. The verse displays the partnership: mutual. The support displays the Philippians alone aiding Paul early. The verse displays the church: only theirs. The verse displays the giving: material. The verse displays the receiving: spiritual. The Philippians alone supporting Paul from the first displays the singularity of their generosity: from the very beginning of the gospel among them, when no other church entered into partnership with him in giving and receiving, they alone supported him — a unique and steadfast fellowship. The verse displays their early faithfulness, repeated even at Thessalonica (v.16).
16:
"For even in Thessalonica ye sent once and again unto my necessity." The brief verse notes the Philippians' repeated gifts even at Thessalonica. The grammatical voices note the early instance. Barnes and Robertson note Paul cites a specific proof of their early generosity: "even in Thessalonica (while he was there, soon after leaving Philippi) ye sent once and again (more than once, repeatedly) unto my necessity (to meet my need)" — their care reached him even there.

On "once and again," the witnesses note the repetition. Barnes and JFB note the phrase "once and again" means repeatedly, more than once — their giving was not a single act but a recurring care; they sent again and again to supply his wants.

The grammatical-historical note the proof: this specific recollection confirms the singular faithfulness just claimed — from the very first, and repeatedly, the Philippians supplied Paul's need. The verse displays the Philippians' repeated gifts even at Thessalonica.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul cites a specific proof — "even in Thessalonica (soon after leaving Philippi) ye sent once and again (repeatedly) unto my necessity," their care reaching him there (Barnes, Robertson); "once and again" meaning repeatedly, a recurring care not a single act (Barnes, JFB); this confirming their singular faithfulness from the first (grammatical-historical). The verse displays their repeated gifts. The verse marks the place: even Thessalonica. The verse displays the repetition: once and again. The verse displays the need: his necessity. The verse displays the proof: singular faithfulness. The gifts display the Philippians' recurring care for Paul. The verse displays the Thessalonica: reached. The verse displays the sending: repeated. The verse displays the necessity: supplied. The Philippians' repeated gifts even at Thessalonica display the proof of their early and steadfast generosity: while Paul was at Thessalonica, soon after leaving them, they sent again and again to meet his need — a recurring care that confirms their unique faithfulness. The verse displays their repeated bounty, which Paul valued for their sake (v.17). What the apostle recalls is no small thing, for it shows that the bond between him and this church was forged in the earliest and hardest days of his Macedonian labour, when help was scarce and he stood almost alone; and the repeated sending, twice over to a single city, marks a generosity that was neither grudging nor occasional but the steady habit of a people who had made his necessities their own.
17:
"Not because I desire a gift: but I desire fruit that may abound to your account." The brief verse notes Paul desiring not the gift but their fruit and reward. The grammatical voices note the disclaimer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul again guards his motive: "not because I desire a gift (it is not the present itself I am after, nor do I value their love for what I receive)" — he is not seeking their money.

On "I desire fruit that may abound to your account," the witnesses note his true desire. Barnes and Calvin note the commercial figure continued: "I desire fruit (the spiritual benefit, the reward of their giving) that may abound (increase, accumulate) to your account (to your credit, as in a ledger)" — Paul rejoices in their gift chiefly because of the spiritual fruit and reward it brings to them.

The grammatical-historical note the selflessness: Paul cares more for their spiritual profit than for his own relief; their giving enriches them, laying up reward to their account with God. The verse displays Paul desiring not the gift but their fruit.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul guards his motive — "not because I desire a gift (not seeking the present itself)," not after their money (Barnes, Robertson); his true desire "fruit (spiritual benefit, reward) that may abound (accumulate) to your account (credit, as in a ledger)," rejoicing in the gift for the reward it brings them (Barnes, Calvin); caring more for their spiritual profit than his relief (grammatical-historical). The verse displays desiring their fruit. The verse marks the disclaimer: not the gift. The verse displays the desire: fruit to their account. The verse displays the figure: a ledger of credit. The verse displays the selflessness: their profit. The desire displays Paul seeking their reward, not his relief. The verse displays the gift: not sought. The verse displays the fruit: desired. The verse displays the account: theirs. Paul desiring not the gift but their fruit and reward displays the selflessness of his thankfulness: he values their bounty not for the relief it brings him but for the spiritual fruit and reward it lays up to their account — caring more for their profit than his own need. The verse displays the reward of their giving, more than meeting his need (v.18).
18:
"But I have all, and abound: I am full, having received of Epaphroditus the things which were sent from you, an odour of a sweet smell, a sacrifice acceptable, wellpleasing to God." The witnesses note their gift as a sacrifice acceptable and well-pleasing to God. On "I have all, and abound: I am full," the grammatical voices note the receipt. Barnes and Robertson note Paul acknowledges full satisfaction: "I have all (I have received everything, my receipt is in full) and abound (have more than enough); I am full (completely supplied), having received of Epaphroditus the things which were sent from you" — their gift, brought by Epaphroditus, met his need abundantly.

On "an odour of a sweet smell, a sacrifice acceptable, wellpleasing to God," the witnesses note the offering. Barnes and Calvin note the sacrificial figure (drawn from the Levitical offerings): their gift is "an odour of a sweet smell (a fragrant aroma), a sacrifice acceptable, wellpleasing to God" — their charity to Paul was, in effect, an offering presented to God himself, fragrant and acceptable to him.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the dignity: a gift to God's servant, given in love, rises to God as a sweet-smelling sacrifice; their kindness was worship. The verse displays their gift as a sacrifice acceptable to God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul acknowledges full satisfaction — "I have all (received in full) and abound; I am full, having received of Epaphroditus the things sent from you," their gift meeting his need abundantly (Barnes, Robertson); the sacrificial figure "an odour of a sweet smell, a sacrifice acceptable, wellpleasing to God," their charity an offering presented to God, fragrant and acceptable (Barnes, Calvin); a gift to God's servant rising to God as worship (Wesley). The verse displays the gift a sacrifice. The verse marks the receipt: I have all. The verse displays the supply: I am full. The verse displays the offering: a sweet-smelling sacrifice. The verse displays the acceptance: well-pleasing to God. The gift displays their charity an offering acceptable to God. The verse displays the fulness: supplied. The verse displays the sacrifice: fragrant. The verse displays the God: well-pleased. Their gift as a sacrifice acceptable and well-pleasing to God displays the high worth Paul sets on their kindness: their bounty, brought by Epaphroditus and abundantly meeting his need, was no mere present but an offering presented to God himself — a fragrant, acceptable, well-pleasing sacrifice. The verse displays the worship of their giving, met by God's promised supply (v.19).
19:
"But my God shall supply all your need according to his riches in glory by Christ Jesus." The witnesses note the promise that God will supply all their need. On "my God shall supply all your need," the grammatical voices note the assurance. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's confident promise in return for their generosity: "my God (the God I serve and know) shall supply (fill up, fully meet) all your need (every want, spiritual and temporal)" — as they had supplied his need, God would supply theirs.

On "according to his riches in glory," the witnesses note the measure. Barnes and Calvin note the boundless standard: "according to his riches in glory (the measure of his own infinite, glorious wealth, not merely out of it but in proportion to it)" — God supplies not sparingly but according to the immeasurable riches of his glory.

On "by Christ Jesus," the grammatical-historical note the channel: this supply comes "by (through, in) Christ Jesus" — all God's provision reaches the believer through Christ, the channel of every blessing. The verse displays the promise that God will supply all their need.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's confident promise — "my God (whom I serve) shall supply (fully meet) all your need (every want)," as they supplied his, God would supply theirs (Barnes, Robertson); "according to his riches in glory," in proportion to his own infinite, glorious wealth, not sparingly (Barnes, Calvin); "by Christ Jesus," the supply reaching them through Christ (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promise of supply. The verse marks the supply: all your need. The verse displays the source: my God. The verse displays the measure: his riches in glory. The verse displays the channel: by Christ Jesus. The promise displays God meeting their every need richly through Christ. The verse displays the need: supplied. The verse displays the riches: glorious. The verse displays the Christ: the channel. The promise that God will supply all their need displays the gracious return for their generosity: the God Paul serves will fully meet their every want — not meagrely but according to the immeasurable riches of his glory, through Christ Jesus. The verse displays God's abundant supply, crowned with a doxology (v.20).
20:
"Now unto God and our Father be glory for ever and ever. Amen." The brief verse notes the doxology of glory to God forever. The grammatical voices note the praise. Barnes and Robertson note that the thought of God's glorious riches (v.19) overflows into a doxology: "unto God and our Father (God, who is our Father) be glory (praise, honour, the ascription of all glory) for ever and ever" — Paul ascribes everlasting glory to God.

On "for ever and ever," the witnesses note the eternity. Barnes and Calvin note the unending duration: "for ever and ever (unto the ages of the ages)" — the glory ascribed to God is to be without end, eternal and perpetual.

On "Amen," the puritan Henry notes the seal: "Amen" — so let it be; the heart's assent and confirmation of the praise. The verse displays the doxology of glory to God forever.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the thought of God's glorious riches overflows into doxology — "unto God and our Father (who is our Father) be glory (praise, honour) for ever and ever," ascribing everlasting glory to God (Barnes, Robertson); "for ever and ever (unto the ages of the ages)," glory without end (Barnes, Calvin); "Amen," the heart's assent and confirmation (Henry). The verse displays the doxology. The verse marks the praise: glory to God. The verse displays the relation: our Father. The verse displays the duration: for ever and ever. The verse displays the seal: Amen. The doxology displays Paul ascribing eternal glory to God. The verse displays the glory: ascribed. The verse displays the Father: ours. The verse displays the eternity: forever. The doxology of glory to God forever displays the overflow of praise that crowns the promise of supply: contemplating the riches of God's glory, Paul ascribes everlasting glory to God, who is our Father, sealing it with Amen. The verse displays the praise of God, before the closing greetings (vv.21-23). The doxology rises naturally out of the promise just made, for having spoken of the riches of God in glory the apostle cannot but break into praise of the God who possesses them; and the ascription of glory for ever and ever, sealed with the Amen, gathers up the whole letter of joy into a single act of worship, owning that from this God, the Father of his people, all supply flows and to him all glory returns.
21:
"Salute every saint in Christ Jesus. The brethren which are with me greet you." The brief verse notes the greeting to every saint and from the brethren. The grammatical voices note the salutation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's closing greetings: "salute (greet) every saint in Christ Jesus" — he sends his greeting to each individual believer, "every saint," omitting none; his love embraces all.

On "every saint," the witnesses note the breadth. Barnes and Ellicott note the comprehensive "every saint" — Paul would have his greeting reach each member of the church, not the leaders only but all the holy ones in Christ.

On "the brethren which are with me greet you," the grammatical-historical note the senders: "the brethren which are with me (Paul's companions at Rome) greet you" — those with him join in the greeting, a token of the fellowship binding the churches. The verse displays the greeting to every saint and from the brethren.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's closing greeting — "salute (greet) every saint in Christ Jesus," his greeting to each believer, omitting none (Barnes, Robertson); the comprehensive "every saint," reaching each member not the leaders only (Barnes, Ellicott); "the brethren which are with me greet you," his companions joining, a token of fellowship (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the greetings. The verse marks the greeting: every saint. The verse displays the breadth: each member. The verse displays the senders: the brethren with him. The verse displays the fellowship: of the churches. The greeting displays Paul's love embracing every saint. The verse displays the saints: greeted. The verse displays the brethren: greeting. The verse displays the fellowship: shared. The greeting to every saint and from the brethren displays Paul's all-embracing love: he sends his salutation to each individual believer, every saint in Christ Jesus, and conveys the greeting of all the brethren with him — a token of the fellowship that binds the churches. The verse displays the closing greetings, widened to Caesar's household (v.22). The comprehensiveness of the greeting is itself a mark of the heart of Paul, for he will not let his salutation rest on the prominent or the leaders only but reaches out to every single one of the holy ones at Philippi; and the joining of the brethren who were with him at Rome turns the simple greeting into a visible token of that wider fellowship which binds together, across all distance, the scattered members of the one body of Christ.
22:
"All the saints salute you, chiefly they that are of Caesar's household." The brief verse notes the greeting from all the saints, especially Caesar's household. The grammatical voices note the wider greeting. Barnes and Robertson note that beyond his immediate companions, "all the saints (all the believers at Rome) salute you" — the whole Roman church sends its greeting to the Philippians.

On "chiefly they that are of Caesar's household," the witnesses note the remarkable detail. Barnes and Calvin note the striking note: "chiefly (especially) they that are of Caesar's household" — there were believers even among the servants and officials of the imperial palace (Nero's household); the gospel had penetrated to the very court of Caesar.

The grammatical-historical note the triumph: that there were saints in Caesar's own household shows how the gospel had spread even amid the imperial court, a token of its power; and it confirms (with 1:13) that the bonds reached the palace. The verse displays the greeting from all the saints, especially Caesar's household.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "all the saints (the whole Roman church) salute you," sending greeting to the Philippians (Barnes, Robertson); the striking "chiefly they that are of Caesar's household," believers even among the servants and officials of the imperial palace, the gospel reaching Caesar's court (Barnes, Calvin); a token of the gospel's spread amid the imperial household (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the wide greeting. The verse marks the senders: all the saints. The verse displays the special: Caesar's household. The verse displays the spread: the imperial court. The verse displays the triumph: the gospel's reach. The greeting displays the gospel penetrating even Caesar's house. The verse displays the saints: all. The verse displays the household: of Caesar. The verse displays the reach: to the court. The greeting from all the saints, especially Caesar's household, displays the remarkable spread of the gospel: the whole Roman church greets the Philippians, and chiefly the believers in Caesar's own household — proof that the gospel had penetrated even to the imperial court. The verse displays the gospel in Caesar's house, sealed by the grace of Christ (v.23).
23:
"The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all. Amen." The witnesses note the closing benediction of grace upon them all. On "the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the blessing. Barnes and Robertson note Paul closes, as he began (1:2), with grace: "the grace (free favour, the whole blessing) of our Lord Jesus Christ" — the benediction that seals the letter.

On "be with you all," the witnesses note the breadth. Barnes and Ellicott note the comprehensive "with you all" — Paul's parting blessing embraces the whole church, every member, without exception; grace for all.

On "Amen," the eastern Cyril notes the seal: "Amen" — the heart's confirmation, committing them to the grace of Christ. The verse displays the closing benediction of grace upon them all.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul closes, as he began, with grace — "the grace (free favour) of our Lord Jesus Christ," the benediction sealing the letter (Barnes, Robertson); "be with you all," embracing the whole church, every member, grace for all (Barnes, Ellicott); "Amen," the heart's confirmation (Cyril). The verse displays the benediction. The verse marks the blessing: the grace of Christ. The verse displays the breadth: with you all. The verse displays the seal: Amen. The verse displays the close: of the epistle. The benediction displays grace invoked on the whole church. The verse displays the grace: of Christ. The verse displays the all: embraced. The verse displays the close: sealed. The closing benediction of grace upon them all displays the seal of the epistle of joy: Paul, as he began, invokes the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ upon the whole church — every member, without exception — sealing the letter with Amen. The verse displays the grace of Christ that closes the epistle to the Philippians, the letter of joy. It is fitting that the letter which opened with grace should close with grace, for grace is the first word and the last of the whole gospel, the fountain from which every blessing named in between has flowed; and the apostle leaves his beloved Philippians, as he found them, commended to the free favour of the Lord Jesus Christ, that same favour being the only ground on which any of them, or any believer since, can stand and rejoice to the end.
